items.mistake: Transposing rows and columns when reading the matrix
items.fix: State explicitly: sklearn uses rows = actual, columns = predicted. Verify with a tiny hand-checked example before quoting TP/FP/TN/FN.
items.why: Swapping axes mislabels FP as FN (or vice versa) — every derived rate becomes wrong.
items.example: Matrix [[5,2],[1,4]] with labels [0,1]: TN=5, FP=2, FN=1, TP=4 — not TP=5.

items.mistake: Computing the confusion matrix on training predictions
items.fix: Build the matrix only on held-out validation or test labels the model did not train on.
items.why: Training counts are optimistically biased — deployment error rates will look worse than reported.
items.example: Wrong: confusion_matrix(y_train, clf.predict(X_train)). Right: same on y_test, X_test.

items.mistake: Reporting accuracy alone on imbalanced data
items.fix: Always show TP, FN (and TPR/recall) for the minority positive class alongside accuracy.
items.why: 99% negatives can yield 99% accuracy with FN capturing every positive — the matrix exposes the miss count.
items.example: Fraud 0.5% rate: accuracy 99.5% but FN=50/50 positives caught = 0% TPR — matrix shows the failure.

items.fix: Document positive class = 1 (e.g. disease present, fraud, spam) before interpreting TP vs TN.
items.why: Reversing positive/negative swaps TP↔TN and FP↔FN — metrics change meaning.
items.example: Spam=1: FP = ham marked spam. If spam=0 instead, the same errors land in different cells.
